text: The surgeon injects diluted vasopressin around the nine-centimeter myoma located in the posterior wall of the uterus, causing the tissue to appear pale due to transient ischemia. Using a harmonic scalpel, the serous layer is incised longitudinally, enlarging the incision to facilitate enucleation of the myoma.

The surgeon uses a myoma screw from the right hand to facilitate the enucleation of the myoma, while holding a traumatic grasper and suction irrigation in the left hand. The myoma is gradually separated using suction irrigation, with energy sources applied to detach dense attachments near the base where feeding vessels are present.

The surgeon completes the separation of the last attachment of the myoma. Suturing is performed using a 40 mm needle to take deep bites through the muscle, closing the incision with extracorporeal knots, such as Mishra's or square knots.

The surgeon ties multiple extracorporeal knots to provide traction and tension, effectively controlling bleeding. A square knot is used to tighten the suture, followed by additional knots to secure the repair. The procedure concludes with morselation to remove the entire myoma.
